title: Seductive Language for Narcissists in Job Postings
abstract: Prior research indicates that narcissistic executives engage in earnings management and other negative organizational behaviors, and many studies ponder why firms hire such individuals, especially into corporate accounting positions. Utilizing a selection of terms from real-world job postings that we characterize as describing either a “Rule-Bender” or “Rule-Follower” candidate, we first conduct several validation studies which reveal that these terms vary predictably across types of job postings, that people generally agree with our categorization of these terms, and that Rule-Benders are viewed as possessing worse managerial skills but a higher proclivity for unethical behavior. We then demonstrate that narcissistic job seekers are more attracted to job postings that describe the ideal candidate using Rule-Bender terms for both general positions (Experiment 1) and senior accounting positions (Experiment 2). Finally, we examine firm characteristics that might lead professional recruiters to incorporate Rule-Bender language into Chief Accounting Officer job postings and find that Rule-Bender terms are preferred for higher-growth, higher-innovation firms (Experiment 3), and when more aggressive reporting would benefit the firm (Experiment 4). Our results suggest that recruiters’ language choices can attract Rule-Bending narcissists to firms, perhaps even in unintended circumstances.

author.Jonathan Gay: Patterson School of Accountancy, University of Mississippi, Oxford, Mississippi 37677
author.Scott B. Jackson: Darla Moore School of Business, University of South Carolina, Columbia, South Carolina 29208
author.Nicholas Seybert: Robert H. Smith School of Business, University of Maryland, College Park, Maryland 20742

article.1. Introduction: Prior research indicates that narcissistic executives are more likely to engage in unethical behavior, including aggressive reporting and earnings management (Rijsenbilt and Commandeur 2013,Olsen et al. 2014,Majors 2016,Ham et al. 2017,Buchholz et al. 2020,Abdel-Meguid et al. 2021), as well as a variety of other harmful organizational practices (Duchon and Drake 2009,Amernic and Craig 2010,Ham et al. 2018,Van Scotter and Roglio 2020). Organizational behavior and applied psychology research finds that narcissists rise to leadership positions partly because they present themselves with confidence and authority, despite their accompanying negative traits (Hogan et al. 1990;Paulhus 1998;Brunell et al. 2008;Goncalo et al. 2010;Nevicka et al. 2011a,b;Ong et al. 2016). This study focuses on the earliest stage at which narcissistic candidates could be attracted to firms by investigating how narcissistic job seekers respond to certain terms in job postings and the firm characteristics that lead recruiters to utilize such terms. Extant literature suggests that narcissists overestimate their own performance (Robins and John 1997) and view themselves as more competent and self-reliant (Raskin and Hall 1981,Paulhus 1998), more creative (Barron and Harrington 1981,Goncalo et al. 2010), and superior to others (Raskin and Hall 1981,Raskin and Terry 1988). In addition, narcissists are more likely to take risks to achieve results (Chatterjee and Hambrick 2007,Buyl et al. 2017,Ham et al. 2018), and also engage in various forms of persuasion to convince others of their superiority (Raskin and Terry 1988;Nevicka et al. 2011a,b;Ham et al. 2024). Based on these previously identified characteristics, we examine postings for senior accounting positions on popular recruiting websites (seeAppendix Afor examples) and identify a set of recurring terms that would appeal to narcissistic job seekers, including phrases and words such as “thinks outside the box,” “flexible and strategic,” “develops creative solutions,” “persuasive,” “ambitious and self-reliant,” and “results-oriented.” Because these terms seem to identify someone who pushes boundaries, we characterize an ideal candidate possessing these traits as a “Rule-Bender.” We then establish a set of opposing traits that we characterize as a “Rule-Follower,” including phrases and words such as “thinks methodically,” “concrete and practical,” “relies on time-tested solutions,” “straightforward,” “grounded and collaborative,” and “process-oriented.” This creates six pairs of candidate descriptors that form a coherent profile of a Rule-Bender versus a Rule-Follower (seeAppendix Bfor the descriptor pairs and full candidate profiles used throughout our studies). We expect that narcissistic job seekers will be more attracted to job postings that describe the ideal candidate as a Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower). We also investigate the firm characteristics and motives that can lead recruiters to incorporate Rule-Bender language into Chief Accounting Officer (CAO) job postings, first focusing on growth and innovation. Prior research suggests the high-growth firms benefit from employees who are flexible, ambitious, creative, and goal-oriented (Kotter and Sathe 1978,Nicholls-Nixon 2005). Research on innovative companies suggests that individuals who prefer innovative company cultures with less rule-oriented environments are more likely to take risks (Amabile 1988,O’Reilly et al. 1991). Finally, high-growth and innovative companies have been shown to exhibit greater earnings management (Lev 2003,Cheng and Warfield 2005,Kwon and Yin 2006,Gleason et al. 2008,Ettredge et al. 2010,Shust 2015,Guggenmos 2020). We therefore expect that recruiters will more strongly prefer to utilize Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower) language in CAO job postings for a higher-growth, higher-innovation firm. Next, we examine an accounting context in which Rule-Bending can be beneficial—the need for aggressive reporting. We expect that executive recruiters will more strongly prefer to utilize Rule-Bender language in job postings for a firm that would benefit from aggressive financial reporting. We initially conduct a series of validation studies to explore and verify the nature and perception of Rule-Bender terms in job postings. First, Boolean searches ofIndeed.comreveal that Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower terms vary predictably across industries (more versus less creative/innovative), job titles (sales-oriented versus compliance-oriented), and firm motives (growth/profit versus established/nonprofit). Second, we conduct three online studies to determine how people view Rule-Bender terms and candidates who possess these traits. In the first study, when provided with each of our candidate descriptor pairs, participants agree that Rule-Bending employees are more likely to possess the traits that we characterize as Rule-Bender terms. In the second and third studies, when viewing full candidate profiles, participants view Rule-Benders as more likely to possess a variety of poorer management skills/attributes but more likely to engage in unethical behaviors including earnings management. In our main experiments, we examine both sides of language in job postings, investigating whether narcissistic job seekers are more attracted to positions that include Rule-Bender terms, and the firm characteristics which lead professional recruiters to utilize such terms in CAO job postings. Experiment 1 draws U.S. college graduates from CloudResearch Connect to evaluate a hypothetical job posting. We utilize a 1 × 2 between-participants design, manipulating the ideal candidate description in the job advertisement (Rule-Bender versus Rule-Follower) between participants, and measuring narcissism using the 16-item narcissistic personality inventory (NPI-16) (Ames et al. 2006). Participants rate the extent to which the advertised job draws on their own talents, skills, and competencies. We predict and find that participants with higher trait narcissism (compared with those with lower trait narcissism) indicate that the position where the ideal candidate is described as a Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower) is a better match for them. Experiment 2 investigates whether the behavior observed in Experiment 1 also applies to experienced accounting managers seeking a CAO position in a setting where two attractive high-tech company jobs are presented side by side. We utilize a comparative design, where one job advertisement describes the ideal candidate as a Rule-Bender and the other job advertisement describes the ideal candidate as a Rule-Follower. We find that accounting managers with higher trait narcissism (compared with those with lower trait narcissism) rate themselves as more likely to apply to, and view themselves as a better fit for, the CAO position where the ideal candidate is described as a Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower). Having established that narcissistic job seekers are attracted to Rule-Bender language, we next investigate the firm characteristics that lead recruiters to incorporate such language into job postings. Experiment 3 utilizes professional recruiters in a 1 × 2 between-participants design, manipulating the type of firm for which they draft a Chief Accounting Officer job posting (a high-growth, high-innovation firm versus a low-growth, low-innovation firm), while holding the job responsibilities of the CAO constant. The descriptions of the companies exclude any mention of terminology from our Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower profiles. Recruiters rate their preference between each of our six pairs of Rule-Bender versus Rule-Follower terms for use in the job posting. We predict and find that recruiters exhibit a stronger preference for Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower) descriptors when the firm is described as high growth and high innovation (as opposed to low growth, low innovation). Finally, Experiment 4 utilizes professional recruiters with significant experience recruiting accounting and finance executives in a 1 × 2 within-participants design, manipulating the purpose for which the firm uses financial statements (monitoring versus valuation), while holding the job responsibilities of the CAO constant. We predict and find that experienced executive recruiters exhibit a stronger preference for Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) descriptors when financial statements are used by prospective investors to value the firm (i.e., when aggressive reporting would benefit the owners) than when financial statements are used by owners to monitor firm operations (i.e., when transparency would benefit owners). Our results contribute to several streams of research. First, we shed light on how the typical recruiting process described in management and personnel psychology literature (e.g.,Chatman 1989,Cable and Judge 1997,Edwards 2008) could have unintended effects when recruiters’ language choices attract narcissistic candidates to firms. Indeed, practitioner publications suggest that recruiters actively avoid hiring narcissists and recognize the potential downsides (Farnsworth and Ella 2015,Stillman 2020,Betz 2023). Second, we expand upon prior psychology and organizational behavior research that questions how narcissists rise to leadership positions (e.g.,Hogan et al. 1990;Paulhus 1998;Brunell et al. 2008;Nevicka et al. 2011a,b;Ong et al. 2016), by showing that the language in job postings may already bias organizations toward attracting a higher proportion of narcissists to the applicant pool (or, conversely, dissuade applicants who are nonnarcissists from entering the applicant pool). Even if subsequent interviews and assessments are conducted fairly and with no preference given to narcissistic candidates, a larger pool of narcissists leads to a higher probability that one will be hired. Third, our results indicate that recruiters prefer Rule-Bender terminology for a younger, higher-growth, higher-innovation firm. This provides one potential explanation for why high-growth and high-innovation firms are found to engage in greater earnings management (Lev 2003,Cheng and Warfield 2005,Kwon and Yin 2006,Gleason et al. 2008,Ettredge et al. 2010,Shust 2015,Guggenmos 2020). Although person-organization fit may be maximized when narcissistic individuals are matched with high-growth and high-innovation firms, there may be downside problems when job postings attract narcissistic individuals for the accounting function. For example, the accounting function often serves as a check on management optimism and opportunistic behavior. This important role may be undermined when the individuals who ascend to positions of power and authority in the accounting function do not fully embrace their oversight responsibilities. Fourth, our results pertain to the importance of personnel controls and the lack thereof—more specifically, control over the employee selection process (Merchant and Van der Stede 2012). Prior literature indicates that personnel controls are particularly important in areas where accounting controls may not be as effective (Rockness and Shields 1988,Abernethy and Stoelwinder 1995,Abernethy and Brownell 1997). Because research suggests that narcissists are more likely to subvert accounting controls, job postings that attract narcissists to accounting positions are indicative of a potential simultaneous breakdown of both personnel controls and accounting controls. Fifth, although a large body of accounting and finance research examines tone and language in various disclosures (Li 2008;Hales et al. 2011;Loughran and McDonald 2011;Rogers et al. 2011;Rennekamp 2012;Huang et al. 2014;Tan et al. 2014,2019;Asay et al. 2018;Loftus and Tanlu 2018), this stream of literature has not considered language in job postings. We believe that the implications of narcissists’ attraction to Rule-Bender terminology are especially unique for accounting positions given the potential for earnings manipulation or other forms of financial malfeasance (Levitt 1998,Dechow and Skinner 2000). Finally, our study complements research on employee selection for accounting positions.Harris et al. (2022)conduct experiments focusing on employee selection, finding that experienced executives and recruitment professionals favor hiring the accounting job candidate who has dark personality traits over the otherwise better qualified job candidate when the hiring organization faces pressure to manage earnings. The complementary issue that we examine is employee attraction to high-level accounting positions. For earnings management to propagate, not only must organizations favor job candidates who display traits indicative of earnings management, but those organizations must attract applications from job candidates who are willing to engage in earnings management. Our evidence complements prior research byHarris et al. (2022)by showing that selection and attraction operate in tandem with one another to yield seemingly undesirable hiring decisions. The remainder of this paper proceeds as follows.Section 2develops our main research hypotheses.Section 3briefly discusses four validation studies (details and results available in the Online Appendix) aimed at determining the prevalence and nature of Rule-Bending language in job postings as well as the perceptions and expectations people have for Rule-Benders.Sections 4and5present Experiments 1 and 2, respectively, which investigate how job seekers are attracted to Rule-Bending language.Sections 6and7present Experiments 3 and 4, respectively, which investigate the firm characteristics and incentives leading recruiters to utilize Rule-Bending language.Section 8provides a conclusion and suggestions for future research in this area.
article.2. Hypothesis Development: 2.1. Word Choices in Job Postings and Candidate Attraction An applicant’s perceived person-organization fit is an important factor in whether a match between an applicant and an organization occurs (Uggerslev et al. 2012). The person-organization fit model suggests that the compatibility of personality traits between a potential applicant and organization will likely influence the potential applicant’s level of attraction to that organization (Kristof 1996,Kristof-Brown et al. 2005). Narcissists’ perceptions of themselves and related actions have been well documented throughout management, accounting, and personnel literature. For instance, narcissists believe they are more creative than others and are also able to persuade others to accept this claim, even though it is false (Barron and Harrington 1981,Farwell and Wohlwend-Lloyd 1998,Goncalo et al. 2010). Narcissists also engage in a variety of persuasive tactics to achieve their desired outcomes (Raskin and Terry 1988;Nevicka et al. 2011a,b;Ham et al. 2024). In turn, narcissistic candidates will likely be attracted to a job posting that includes terms that signal a need for someone creative (e.g.,Appendix ARule-Bender excerpts 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 7) and persuasive (e.g.,Appendix ARule-Bender excerpts 1, 5, 7, 8, 12, and 14). Alongside self-proclaimed creativity, narcissists possess a strong desire for admiration and success, which often leads to a push for action in the form of innovation or risk-taking to reach goals.Cragun et al. (2020)examine prior literature on CEO narcissism in a meta-analysis to understand how CEO narcissism impacts an organization and financial performance. They find that a significant positive relationship between CEO narcissism and innovation exists in prior literature (Chatterjee and Hambrick 2007,Ingersoll et al. 2017). Prior research also suggests that CEOs are more willing to make risky decisions (Chatterjee and Hambrick 2007,Buyl et al. 2017,Ham et al. 2018) and manipulate financial information to reach goals (Rijsenbilt and Commandeur 2013,Olsen et al. 2014,Ham et al. 2017). Although an increased desire and willingness to innovate, take risks, and manipulate company policies are the means by which narcissists feed their desire for success and admiration, these types of behaviors can only manifest when given an opportunity. We expect that job postings that reflect opportunities for high levels of success and admiration would likely result in a perceived person-organization fit by a narcissistic candidate. For instance, following the person-organization fit model, job postings that communicate a desire for individuals who are ambitious and striving for results (e.g.,Appendix ARule-Bender excerpts 6, 8, 9, 16, 17, and 18) and promote innovation (e.g.,Appendix ARule-Bender excerpts 3, 7, 9, 11, 15, and 20) are likely to appeal to narcissistic candidates. Finally, the unique implications of the combination of Rule-Bender terms for accounting positions, which we expect (as predicted in our first hypothesis) communicates a potential demand for pushing ethical boundaries, also correspond to several general facets of trait narcissism.Raskin and Terry (1988)include authority, superiority, entitlement, and exploitativeness as four of the seven dimensions of narcissism. This combination of personality traits seems particularly well suited to a job where there is a need for pushing boundaries. Therefore, in addition to our previous discussion of specific individual terms and prior research results concerning the behavior of narcissists, individuals who are exploitative and entitled, and possess notions of superiority and authority over others, are likely to be more generally drawn to jobs that demand bending of the rules. Based on previous literature examining narcissists’ behavior and beliefs, we expect that job seekers with higher levels of trait narcissism will be more attracted to job postings with Rule-Bender terms relative to Rule-Follower terms. This expectation is formally stated in our first hypothesis: Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in job postings are more attractive to job seekers as trait narcissism increases. 2.2. Recruiters’ Word Choices in Job Postings As previously noted, management and personnel psychology researchers have long recognized that attracting and matching applicants to jobs are of paramount importance for organizational success (Rynes and Barber 1990). One critical factor in attracting applicants involves communicating appropriate and relevant information about the organization, job, and ideal candidate. Prior research recognizes the importance of providing realistic information about what jobs entail (Philips 1998) and finds that in addition to considering obvious factors like location, salary, and benefits (Barber and Roehling 1993), fit with the organizational culture and industry is a top consideration for job applicants (Cable and Graham 2000).Turban and Keon (1993)find that candidates with different personality profiles have different preferences regarding organization size, decentralization, compensation, and other features of the job. A large stream of literature addresses the importance of person-organization fit, or the perceived congruence among the organization’s culture, values, goals, and norms with those of the candidate (Kristof 1996), in matching individuals to firms (Schneider 1987,Chatman 1989,Rynes and Gerhart 1990,Cable and Judge 1997,Edwards 2008). In fact, although job and organizational characteristics are important in determining applicant attraction, these factors are almost entirely subsumed by applicants’ perceived person-organization fit (Uggerslev et al. 2012). In addition, prior research indicates that recruiters and firms engage in personality testing on a regular basis to determine applicant fit (Stabile 2002,Krell 2005,Baez 2013). Regular use of personality tests suggests that recruiters understand that certain individual traits will better match with certain position requirements, which indicates that recruiters are likely to pursue person-organization fit for position openings through their recruiting efforts (e.g., job postings). Prior accounting research also addresses the issue of personnel controls, one of which is to ensure that the proper individuals are matched to positions in which they can self-regulate and appropriately behave given the nature of the job (Abernethy and Stoelwinder 1995). Control over employee selection is particularly important when accounting controls may not be sufficient to ensure appropriate behavior (Rockness and Shields 1988,Abernethy and Brownell 1997,Merchant and Van der Stede 2012). It logically follows that individuals with a propensity to subvert accounting controls would present a risk for senior accounting positions when considered from a personnel control perspective. Firms and recruiters may either intentionally or unintentionally subvert control over selection and placement when crafting job postings, although the importance of avoiding narcissists in the hiring process is well known in the practitioner sphere (e.g.,Farnsworth and Ella 2015,Stillman 2020,Betz 2023). A key question is which firm characteristics or motives might cause recruiters to incorporate Rule-Bender terms into their job postings. Prior research in accounting suggests that more earnings management occurs in high-growth and high-innovation firms (Lev 2003,Cheng and Warfield 2005,Kwon and Yin 2006,Gleason et al. 2008,Ettredge et al. 2010,Shust 2015,Guggenmos 2020), suggesting that these firms may have a less rule-oriented culture.Kotter and Sathe (1978)conduct interviews with managers and employees of high-growth firms and suggest that these individuals need to be “flexible” and ambitious to adapt to the constantly changing demands placed on them. CEOs interviewed byNicholls-Nixon (2005)similarly states that employees of high-growth firms must be “self-organizing” and “creative,” and need to set clear “goals,” because high-growth firms tend to be less “process-driven.” Research on innovative firms suggests that individuals who prefer innovative company cultures with less rule-oriented environments are more likely to take risks (Amabile 1988,O’Reilly et al. 1991). In accounting,Guggenmos (2020)manipulates the innovativeness of company culture and finds that individuals assigned to a more (versus less) innovative company culture condition are more likely to participate in real earnings management. Based on this prior research, we predict that professional recruiters will exhibit a stronger preference for utilizing Rule-Bender terms for a higher-growth, higher-innovation firm, as formally specified in our second hypothesis: Professional recruiters include more Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings for a higher-growth, higher-innovation firm (as compared with a lower-growth, lower-innovation firm). In addition to fundamental firm characteristics like growth and innovation, it is possible that recruiters with experience in recruiting accounting and finance executives are sensitive to the need for aggressive financial reporting, or at the very least recognize that a Rule-Bender is more likely to report more aggressively than a Rule-Follower. Although firms are unlikely to come out and clearly specify that they desire an earnings manipulator, these preferences could come through in related firm needs. In our setting, the firm expresses a need either for financial statements that are used for efficiency and monitoring purposes, or for financial statements that are used for valuation purposes to raise additional capital. We expect that executive recruiters will exhibit a stronger preference for Rule-Bender terms as the benefit of aggressive reporting increases, as formally specified in our third hypothesis: Executive recruiters include more Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings when the benefit of aggressive reporting is higher (as compared with lower).
article.3. Validation Studies: The Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower language that comprises the ideal job candidate descriptions shown inAppendix Bis an integral part of the designs of the main experiments reported inSections 4–7. The purpose of the studies reported in this section is to validate the content of the ideal job candidate descriptions. Although more details and results of the validation studies (including tables and figures) are available in the Online Appendix, we briefly summarize the aims and inferences here: The purpose of Validation Study 1 (VS1) is to identify natural variation in language used in job postings across industries, job titles, and company types, consistent with the job candidate descriptions inAppendix B. Results from a Boolean search ofIndeed.comindicate that Rule-Bender terms tend to be used in job postings when the position benefits from innovation, creativity, and outside-the-box thinking, whereas Rule-Follower terms tend to be used in job postings when the position benefits from deliberative thinking, adherence to procedures, and compliance. The purpose of Validation Study 2 (VS2) is to determine whether laypersons associate the phrases comprising (1) the Rule-Bender candidate description with a person who tends to bend rules and (2) the Rule-Follower candidate description with a person who tends to follow rules. Results indicate that the Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower labels seem to fit the respective grouping of items that comprise the ideal candidate descriptions inAppendix B. The purpose of Validation Study 3 (VS3) is to provide evidence that (1) the Rule-Bender is perceived to display a higher propensity to manage earnings than the Rule-Follower and (2) the Rule-Follower is perceived to be a better manager than the Rule-Bender. Results indicate that the candidate description for the Rule-Bender (1) does convey an intended message about a heightened propensity to manage earnings and (2) does not convey an unintendedly positive message about managerial competencies. The purpose of Validation Study 4 (VS4) is to provide evidence that the Rule-Bender language, relative to the Rule-Follower language, conveys information about an elevated potential for general ethical missteps and unethical behavior. Results indicate that the Rule-Bender language is perceived to imply a propensity for unethical behavior. Taken together, the validation studies provide evidence that the candidate descriptions shown inAppendix Bhave both internal and external validity. Having validated that Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower terms occur commonly and heterogeneously across job postings, and that lay observers view these sets of terms as identifying different types of individuals, we next present our four experiments. The first two experiments test the effects of Rule-Bending language on job seekers, whereas the second two experiments test the effects of company characteristics on recruiters’ tendency to use Rule-Bending language.
article.4. Experiment 1: 4.1. Purpose The purpose of our first experiment is to testHypothesis 1, examining whether Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in job postings are more attractive to job seekers as trait narcissism increases. 4.2. Participants We recruit 248 participants using CloudResearch Connect. Participants are required to (1) be 25 years of age or older, (2) have a bachelor’s degree or higher, and (3) speak English as their first language. We compensate participants $1 for participating and offer a $0.50 bonus for correctly answering attention checks. The bonus is meant to incentivize participants to pay careful attention to the experimental materials. Of the 248 participants, 243 correctly answered both attention check questions. Our results include all 248 participants, but our inferences and conclusions are unaffected when we exclude participants who miss an attention check question. 4.3. Demographic Information Participants have an average (median) age of 43 (39) years and an average (median) work experience of 21 (20) years. Although the baseline education requirement was a bachelor’s degree, approximately 30% of participants also possess a master’s degree. About 43.5% of our participants are female. 4.4. Instrument Design The instrument asks participants to imagine that they are in the job search process, and that they have already identified several job advertisements that are a good fit for them based on their personality. The participants then come across an additional advertisement, which lists an ideal candidate profile. We manipulate the ideal candidate profile between participants such that they see either the Rule-Bender or Rule-Follower profile (seeAppendix B). Immediately below the profile, the instrument states, “in the real world, some successful people match the above profile while other successful people do not.” Participants then respond on a 100-point sliding scale to the question “Based on the description of the ideal candidate above, to what extent do you feel that this job would match your own talents, skills, and competencies?” The endpoints of the scale are labeled “very little” (0) and “very much” (100), with the midpoint labeled as “somewhat.” The instrument is silent about the nature of the company and is intentionally very general. Following the central dependent measure, participants then complete a brief distractor task, which is completely unrelated to this study.1Next, participants respond to the 16-item Narcissism Personality Inventory scale developed byAmes et al. (2006). We aggregate responses to the 16 questions, resulting in a participant score that can range from 0 to 16. We code responses such that higher values indicate greater narcissistic tendencies. Finally, participants answer several demographic questions. 4.5. Results The mean (median) NPI-16 score for participants is 3.94 (3.00). The 10th percentile score is 0 and the 90th percentile score is 9.2Further, the first quartile score is 0 and 1, the second quartile score is 2 and 3, the third quartile score is 4–6, and the fourth quartile score is 7–16.3Figure 1graphs responses in each condition split by the median (panel (a)) and by quartile of narcissism (panel (b)). Both panel (a) and panel (b) reveal that, consistent withHypothesis 1, the preference for the company with the ideal candidate described using Rule-Bender terms increases between the median and across quartiles of the NPI scale. There is a weaker and nonmonotonic pattern in the opposite direction for the candidate described using Rule-Follower terms. Table 1presents the means using a dichotomous trait narcissism variable, an analysis of variance results (ANOVA), and simple effects. Panel A reports the means of the 2 × 2 ANOVA, which are graphed in panel (a) ofFigure 1. Panel B ofTable 1reports the ANOVA results. The results in panel B and panel C reveal strong statistical evidence that Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in company job postings are more attractive to job seekers as trait narcissism increases. Evidence forHypothesis 1would be observed by a significant interaction, which is strongly significant (F-statistic = 7.71,p< 0.01).4Panel C adds further support by indicating that (1) Rule-Bender language is more attractive to individuals with higher levels of trait narcissism versus lower levels of trait narcissism (F-statistic = 13.36,p< 0.01), (2) individuals with lower levels of trait narcissism view themselves as a better match for jobs with Rule-Follower descriptions (F-statistic = 4.17,p= 0.04), and (3) individuals with higher levels of trait narcissism view themselves as a better match for jobs with Rule-Bender descriptions (F-statistic = 3.57,p= 0.06). Thus, we find support for the hypothesis that Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in job postings are more attractive to job seekers as trait narcissism increases. Table 1.Experiment 1—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 1 Table 1.Experiment 1—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 1 Notes.SeeSection 4for a discussion of Experiment 1. Two hundred forty-eight participants answer the question “Based on the description of the ideal candidate, to what extent do you feel that this job would match your own talents, skills, and competencies?” They respond on a 100-point scale, with the endpoints labeled “very little” (1) and “very much” (100).DESCrepresents our between-participants manipulation, and is a binary variable equal to one if the Rule-Bender profile is presented as the ideal job candidate, and zero if Rule-Follower is presented as the ideal candidate (seeAppendix B). Participants respond to the 16-item NPI scale developed byAmes et al. (2006). We aggregate responses to the 16 questions, resulting in a participant score that can range from 0 to 16. We code responses such that higher values indicate greater narcissistic tendencies and conduct a median split to create two groups (low and high narcissism), labeling this variableNARCin the ANOVA table. aTwo-tailedp-values.
article.5. Experiment 2: 5.1. Purpose The purpose of this experiment is to testHypothesis 1using experienced accounting professionals in the context of comparing two attractive jobs. We use experienced accounting managers to test whether a job posting describing the ideal candidate as a Rule-Bender (as opposed to Rule-Follower) is viewed as more attractive as trait narcissism increases. 5.2. Participant Recruitment To identify potential participants, we use a web-scraping tool to capture the name, email address, location, and employer of individuals who (1) have the title of “accounting manager,” (2) have more than three years of experience, and (3) work in a public company with at least 200 employees.5The web-scraping tool identified approximately 17,125 professionals who met the criteria. We then sent an initial email to the professionals inviting them to participate in an online study (the online platform is Qualtrics). About 3,200 email addresses were returned as undeliverable. Approximately 10 days after our initial email, we sent a follow-up request to each professional. Two hundred forty-six professionals began the experiment, and 154 professionals completed it for a response rate of 1.1% (154/13,925). 5.3. Demographic Information On average (median), participants are about 43 (41) years of age, and they have almost 22 (20) years of total work experience with about 18 (15) of those years in accounting/finance. Around 43% of participants have a bachelor’s degree and 56% have a graduate degree. The Certified Public Accountant (CPA) designation is held by about 67% of our participants, and 12% of them hold a professional designation other than, or in addition to, the CPA. About 58% of participants are female. Turning to job titles, 57% hold the title of manager, 28% hold the title of senior manager, and 15% hold some other title such as director or controller. Managers and senior managers often have specific titles such as “manager of technical accounting” or “manager of SEC reporting.” The demographic information suggests that our participants are well suited for the professional judgment that we ask of them. 5.4. Instrument Design The instrument places participants in the role of an accounting professional who is considering two job opportunities as CAO at two different public companies. Participants are informed that they should assume that their educational and professional experience qualifies them for both positions and that the positions have similar compensation, benefits, and prestige. The instrument then provides a brief description of the CAO’s responsibilities in summary form, which is constant across conditions: Oversee and effectively manage the corporate accounting function Implement and maintain accounting policies, procedures, and internal controls Prepare and review the company’s SEC filings, including 10-K and 10-Q Coordinate the work of company personnel related to annual financial statement audit Oversee the preparation of internal budgets and special reports Consistently meet critical deadlines We elect to provide this detail (and hold it constant across conditions) because we want to avoid any misconception that the CAO’s role involves strategic, innovative, and/or creative endeavors within the company. Instead, the company’s contemplated role for the CAO involves mundane aspects of the accounting function only. Participants must initially respond to a series of questions about each of two job postings, so we provide them with information about the company, position, and type of person (i.e., ideal job candidate) the company wants to hire. The instrument then presents the two ideal candidate descriptions side by side where a company description is matched with one of the two ideal candidate descriptions (i.e., Rule-Bender and Rule-Follower). The company descriptions are designed to be appealing, but they are of no interest experimentally (the company matched to the Rule-Bender versus Rule-Follower is counterbalanced). Both companies are innovative and technology-oriented, and they both have plans to expand in the future. By making the company descriptions cosmetically different but substantively equivalent, we allow the two ideal candidate descriptions to drive job seekers’ preferences. After participants view the companies and ideal candidate descriptions, they respond to the set of dependent variables, which are as follows: Which job opportunity is the best match for your personality? Which job opportunity do you find most interesting? Which job opportunity are you most likely to pursue? Which job opportunity would draw upon your talents, skills, and competencies to the greatest extent? Participants respond on 100-point sliding scales with the left endpoints of 1 labeled with the name of the first company presented to them and the right endpoints of 100 labeled with the name of the second company presented to them. The midpoint of the scale is labeled “No difference.” Responses are coded such that lower values indicate a preference for the candidate who is a Rule-Follower and higher values indicate a preference for the candidate who is a Rule- Bender. Next, we have participants respond to the 16-item NPI scale developed byAmes et al. (2006). We aggregate responses to the 16 questions, resulting in a participant score that can range from 0 to 16.6We code responses such that higher values indicate greater narcissistic tendencies. Finally, we ask participants to answer demographic questions (discussed above). 5.5. Results This experiment uses experienced accounting managers to again testHypothesis 1, which predicts a stronger preference for jobs that use terminology describing the ideal candidate as a Rule-Bender (as compared with a Rule-Follower) as trait narcissism increases. The mean (median) NPI-16 score for participants is 4.61 (4.00). The 10th percentile score is one and the 90th percentile score is eight.7Further, the first quartile score is 0 to 2, the second quartile score is 3 and 4, the third quartile score is 5 and 6, and the fourth quartile score is 7 to 13.8We testHypothesis 1by analyzing whether responses to our dependent measures indicate a Rule-Bender preference as trait narcissism increases. In addition to analyzing the individual questions, we also analyze the average of the individual questions.9 Table 2provides the means for the dependent variables along with means partitioned by quartiles of narcissism. Responses are coded such that lower values are indicative of a preference for the job opportunity in which the ideal candidate is the Rule-Follower whereas higher values are indicative of a preference for the job opportunity in which the ideal candidate is the Rule-Bender. Support forHypothesis 1would be indicated by increases in mean responses across quartiles of narcissism. Consistent withHypothesis 1,Figure 2shows that responses to each question increase monotonically across the quartiles.Table 2reports two tests for each of the four questions. We find that the mean response in quartile 1 is less than the mean response in quartile 4 for each of the four questions and the average of the four questions (p< 0.01). Likewise, we find that the mean responses in quartiles 1 and 2 combined are less than the mean responses in quartiles 3 and 4 combined for each of the four questions and the average of the four questions (p< 0.01). These comparative results provide further support forHypothesis 1. Table 2.Experiment 2—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 1 Table 2.Experiment 2—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 1 Notes.SeeSection 5for a discussion of Experiment 2. This table displays response means and differences by narcissism quartile for each of the comparative dependent measures. One hundred fifty-four experienced accounting managers answer questions on a 100-point response scale where 1 represents the Rule-Follower job description and 100 represents the Rule-Bender job description, with the midpoint labeled as “No difference.” aTwo-tailedp-values.
article.6. Experiment 3: 6.1. Purpose The purpose of this experiment is to testHypothesis 2, which predicts that experienced recruiters include more Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings for a higher-growth, higher-innovation firm (as compared with a lower-growth, lower-innovation firm). 6.2. Participants Participants are comprised of 67 alumni from a top-ranked Master of Human Resource program who currently work as human resource professionals. The director of the program contacted approximately 275 alumni through LinkedIn and requested their participation in an online study. 6.3. Demographic Information The mean (median) age is about 32 (30) years, and the mean (median) work experience is about 10 (8) years. The mean (median) work experience in a human resource capacity is about 7 (6) years. Participants have been involved in about 161 (50) job searches to fill a position. About 64% of participants are female. 6.4. Instrument Design The instrument starts by providing background information to participants. Specifically, we inform participants that they are going to create a job description for a company that is trying to hire a chief accounting officer to oversee the company’s accounting operations. We state that the newly hired chief accounting officer will make judgments that have a significant impact on amounts reported in the company’s financial statements. We also state that different individuals will make different judgments even under the same circumstances, so the amounts reported in the financial statements will vary depending on the person who is hired. Participants then read one of two company descriptions, which is manipulated between participants. We carefully worded the descriptions to convey the notions of innovation and growthwithout utilizing terms included in the Rule-Bender or Rule-Follower candidate descriptions. The higher-growth, higher-innovation company description states the following: The company is a mid-sized, high-technology firm. Due to the introduction of advanced new products, the company has grown its customer base and dramatically expanded its market share and profits. The company faces intense competition from a variety of well-financed competitors. In order to achieve a competitive advantage, the company must quickly respond to emerging consumer demands, offer advanced products and services, and achieve excellent brand recognition. The lower-growth, lower-innovation company description states the following: The company is a mid-sized, mature manufacturing firm. Due to established products, the company has a reliable customer base and stable profits and market share which are unlikely to decline in the future. The company faces little competition due to high barriers to entry. In order to maintain its competitive advantage, the company must control costs, offer products and services that established customers have come to rely upon and trust, and maintain brand recognition. After reading the company description, participants are asked to make choices between six pairs of terms to include in the job posting. The pairs are comprised of the Rule-Follower versus Rule-Bender terms shown inAppendix B. Participants respond on a sliding scale with the left endpoint containing one of the six terms and the right endpoint containing the matched term. The midpoint of the scale is labeled “Indifferent.” The order of the six pairs is randomized, and the appearance of a descriptor is randomized to appear on the left or right endpoint. Responses take on a value of 1 to 100 and are coded so that lower (higher) values indicate a preference for the Rule-Follower (Rule-Bender) terms. Lastly, participants respond to several demographic questions. 6.5. Results Table 3provides experimental results, andFigure 3graphs the results. Visual inspection ofFigure 3suggests that the results comport withHypothesis 2. When the company is described as a higher-growth, higher-innovation firm (as compared with a lower-growth, lower-innovation firm), we find that participants favor Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings. Descriptor pairs 1 and 3 are marginal (t-statistics of 1.94 and 1.77, respectively;p= 0.06 andp= 0.08, respectively), but the remaining descriptor pairs are all significant (t-statistics ≥ 2.31;p≤ 0.02). These results provide support forHypothesis 2.10 Table 3.Experiment 3—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 2 Table 3.Experiment 3—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 2 Notes.SeeSection 6for a discussion of Experiment 3. This table displays the preferences of 67 experienced recruiters for which term in a pair of terms to include in a job posting. The pairs are comprised of the Rule-Follower vs. Rule-Bender terms shown inAppendix B. Participants respond on a sliding scale with the left endpoint containing one of the six terms and the right endpoint containing the matched term. The midpoint of the scale is labeled “Indifferent.” The order of the six pairs is randomized, and the appearance of a descriptor is randomized to appear on the left or right endpoint. Responses take on a value of 0 to 100 and are coded so that lower (higher) values indicate a preference for the Rule-Follower (Rule-Bender) terms. aTwo-tailedp-values.
article.7. Experiment 4: 7.1. Purpose The purpose of this experiment is to testHypothesis 3. We expect to find that executive recruiters include more Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings as the demand for aggressive reporting increases. 7.2. Participant Recruitment In order to identify potential participants, we use a web-scraping tool to capture the name, email address, location, and employer of individuals who (1) have the title of “executive recruiter” and (2) seem to focus on accounting or finance recruitment.11The experiment was completed by 57 executive recruiters. 7.3. Demographic Information The mean (median) age is about 46 (45) years, and the mean (median) work experience is about 25 (25) years with about 15 (15) of those years in recruitment. Participants have been involved in about 733 (500) job searches to fill a position. About 58% of participants are female. 7.4. Instrument Design We place participants in the role of a recruitment professional who is helping to create two job postings for two different private companies seeking to hire a CAO. All participants read the following information: Each newly hired chief accounting officer will prepare financial statements (and various financial reports). The preparation of financial statements often requires the exercise of professional judgment, which could have a significant impact on the amounts reported in the financial statements. Different individuals may make different accounting judgments even under the same circumstances. As a result, amounts reported in the financial statements may differ, depending on the person preparing them. The new CAO will have responsibilities that are common to most CAOs (i.e., preparing financial statements and financial reports). In addition, our manipulation provides additional details about the primary purpose for which the financial statements will be used, which is manipulated at two levels within participants (retaining only the first response to form a between-participants design yields similar results, as discussed in the results section). In the monitoring condition, we state the following: The primary use of the financial statements, as prepared by the newly hired chief accounting officer, will be to monitor company operations. The purpose of monitoring company operations is to prevent inefficiencies in the company’s operations and correct inefficiencies that might occur. By effectively monitoring operations, the owner wants to prevent production from inefficiently using (or wasting) scarce resources. To effectively monitor company operations, the owner needs the financial statements to be informative and transparent about what is actually happening within the company. In the firm valuation condition, we state the following: The primary use of the financial statements, as prepared by the newly hired chief accounting officer, will be to determine the market value of the company. The purpose of valuing the company is to get a large infusion of cash from private equity investors who want to acquire a 10% ownership stake. Independent valuation professionals will determine the market value of the company using information reported in the financial statements, such as net income. To obtain largest possible infusion of cash, the owner is aiming for the highest possible earnings performance, which will result in a high company valuation and a large infusion of cash. Different financial statement purposes may prompt executive recruiters to describe the ideal candidate using different words and phrases. The monitoring condition conveys the notion that job applicants who are transparent and forthright may fit the position best. This condition corresponds to low demand for earnings management. Conversely, the firm valuation condition conveys the idea that job applicants who are comfortable reporting more aggressively may fit the position best. This condition corresponds to high demand for aggressive reporting.12 We made the deliberate decision to include only essential information in the instrument to ensure that we have the cleanest possible manipulation of demand for aggressive reporting. Although information about, for example, the company might add to the mundane realism of the experiment, it introduces noise and possible bias.13Companies in certain industries are known to place unique demands on their employees, which could cause executive recruiters to alter their judgments in response to such factors. Therefore, we describe the purpose for which the financial statements will be used in a way that is largely unrevealing about the company itself. With respect to the monitoring condition, notice that the need for efficient operations spans a broad spectrum of different companies and industries (e.g., food, textiles, apparel, chemical, and computer and electronics). With respect to the firm valuation condition, evidence reveals that private equity transactions occur (1) in a wide variety of industries, (2) at a variety of points in a firm’s life cycle, and (3) in a wide variety of geographic locations (Gompers and Lerner 2000). To elicit the judgments, each Rule-Follower phrase is matched with a Rule-Bender phrase, and the resulting pairs comprise the endpoint labels of six different 101-point scales.14The left and right endpoint labels are randomized across participants but are consistent within participants across the monitoring and valuation conditions to avoid confusion. Responses to the dependent variable are coded so that lower numbers indicate a preference for Rule-Follower phrases and higher numbers indicate a preference for Rule-Bender phrases. We use a repeated-measures design, so each participant chooses between six pairs of phrases for each company. Lastly, participants respond to demographic questions, as discussed above. 7.5. Results Table 4provides descriptive statistics and tests ofHypothesis 3, andFigure 4graphs the mean values. The dependent variable is coded so that lower (higher) responses to the descriptor comparisons indicate a preference for the Rule-Follower (Rule-Bender).Hypothesis 3predicts that executive recruiters will include more Rule-Bender (as compared with Rule-Follower) terms in accounting job postings as the demand for aggressive reporting increases. The cell means shown inTable 4reveal that there is a strong preference for the Rule-Bender over the Rule-Follower when the purpose of financial statements is valuation. All six differences are significant in the predicted direction (t-statistics ≥ 2.26;p≤ 0.03), and the largest difference is for descriptor pair 3 with means of 37.62 and 62.97 in the Rule-Bender versus Rule-Follower conditions, respectively (t-statistic = 3.99;p< 0.01). Because of potential concerns of sensitization or demand-effects resulting from within-participant presentation, we reconduct the analyses using only participants’ first response as a between-participants test. Although this significantly reduces our power with such a small sample, four of the six descriptor pairs remain significant atp< 0.05, two-tailed, as does a composite measure of all six pairs. Taken together, these results indicate an increased preference for the Rule-Bender phrases when demand for aggressive reporting is higher. Table 4.Experiment 4—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 3 Table 4.Experiment 4—Descriptive Statistics and Tests ofHypothesis 3 Notes.SeeSection 7for a discussion of Experiment 4. This table displays 57 experienced executive recruiters’ mean preferences for pairs of terms consistent with either Rule-Follower (0 on the scale) or Rule-Bender (100 on the scale), with the midpoint labeled as “Indifferent.” Financial statement (FS) purpose is manipulated within participants with a stated purpose of either monitoring or valuation. The pairs are comprised of the Rule-Follower vs. Rule-Bender terms shown inAppendix B. aTwo-tailedp-values.
article.8. Conclusion: We report the results of four experiments demonstrating that narcissists are attracted to Rule-Bender language in job postings and that experienced recruiters include such language for higher-growth and -innovation firms, as well as for firms that would benefit from aggressive reporting. Our results contribute to several streams of literature. We contribute to psychology and organizational behavior research questioning how narcissists rise to power (e.g.,Hogan et al. 1990;Paulhus 1998;Brunell et al. 2008;Nevicka et al. 2011a,b;Ong et al. 2016) by demonstrating that job posting language may attract narcissists to leadership positions at the very earliest phases of the recruiting process. We contribute to management and personnel psychology literature examining the recruiting process in organizations (e.g.,Chatman 1989,Cable and Judge 1997,Edwards 2008) by showing that firm characteristics such as growth, innovation, and demand for aggressive reporting can cause recruiters to utilize Rule-Bender language in job postings. We also contribute to accounting research involving managers’ personality traits and misreporting by showing that the narcissism-misreporting link identified in prior research (Olsen et al. 2014,Ham et al. 2017,Abdel-Meguid et al. 2021) could be exacerbated during the recruiting process when Rule-Bender language is used in job postings, and our results may help to explain the increased incidence of misreporting in innovative and growth firms (Lev 2003,Cheng and Warfield 2005,Kwon and Yin 2006,Gleason et al. 2008,Ettredge et al. 2010,Shust 2015,Guggenmos 2020). Finally, we expand upon prior work examining language and tone in disclosures (Li 2008;Hales et al. 2011;Loughran and McDonald 2011;Rogers et al. 2011;Rennekamp 2012;Huang et al. 2014;Tan et al. 2014,2019;Asay et al. 2018) by investigating a new and important communication channel (job postings), and highlighting the role of Rule-Bender versus Rule-Follower language used in the recruiting process. Our results also have practical implications. First, there appears to be a general lack of awareness about how individuals who are predisposed to manage earnings enter corporate hierarchies. Although research has explored ways that earnings management might be mitigated (Klein 2002,Feng et al. 2009,Cheng et al. 2016), little has been done to uncover why earnings management arises in the first place. One common explanation is that earnings management arises, in part, because of various financial incentives facing executives (Healy 1985,Key 1997,Teoh et al. 1998). Although no person is immune to temptation, scholars have begun to address the question: what types of people resist moral and ethical temptations (Bazerman and Sezer 2016,Raval 2018)? Our evidence suggests that the people who may be least able to resist moral and ethical temptations are the ones who find the Rule-Bender language appearing in common accounting job postings most attractive. Relatedly, although the staffing literature in management heavily focuses on fit (e.g., person-organization fit, person-job fit), we wonder if fit should be subordinated to other considerations when seeking to fill high-level accounting positions that often require strong moral fiber and a willingness to make decisions that are unpopular with senior management. Second,Johnson et al. (2021, p. 1)note that there has been a rise in narcissism among client personnel and audit professionals. This observation partly motivated their quasi-experiment which concluded, among other things, that “…auditor narcissism was found to interactively influence client risk inferences such that auditors higher in narcissism exhibited narcissistic tolerance (lower risk assessments) when the hypothetical CFO displayed high verbal narcissism.” At the same time, the findings ofHarris et al. (2022)suggest that individuals who have dark personality traits are more likely to be selected for positions of power and authority in the accounting function of firms. When our findings are considered in tandem with prior research, we see a somewhat troubling confluence of events. Narcissists seem to experience heightened success in the labor market, and such individuals also tend to be most attracted to high-growth and high-innovation firms where oversight skills of accounting professionals are needed most. Auditors may display narcissistic tolerance, so elevated concerns that should accompany interactions with individuals who are predisposed to engage in aggressive and potentially abusive accounting practices may be lacking. As such, ongoing regulatory efforts to halt accounting abuses seem to face a countervailing force in the form of accounting personnel who feel less constrained by rules and regulations. Future research could investigate other types of language used in job postings, including more traditional textual analysis variables such as positive and negative tone. It seems likely that positive tone would attract applicants with a different personality profile than neutral or negative tone. Future studies could also examine the trade-offs that recruiters or firms make when seeking multiple competing personality traits. For example, it is conceivable that a company would seek a candidate willing to bend rules but who is also easygoing and works well with others. How do companies or recruiters screen these competing attributes, and how often do they unexpectedly attract a candidate who conflicts with one important element of company culture in service of another? This study is subject to certain limitations. First, although we have gone to extensive lengths to validate the ideal candidate descriptions shown inAppendix B, we acknowledge that some readers may continue to have questions about the validity of the descriptions. In particular, the creation of the ideal candidate descriptions involved considerable researcher judgment. Our judgments may not comport with the subjective judgments of other scholars. Second, although we went to great lengths to obtain appropriate professional participants, our sample sizes are modest in some instances. The main drawback of modest sample sizes is that power may be insufficient to detect an effect even if present. It is also possible that different inferences could arise if we obtained more professional participants, although this seems unlikely. Third, our experiments involve simplified settings which may omit factors that participants find relevant when making certain judgments. We attempted to incorporate essential features of the environment into our experiments while avoiding mundane realism that would lengthen the time it takes to participate.
